summary: Elizabeth Gilman was one of the many people whose papers I saved because they were personal and related to the work I was doing. She was involved in the circles I moved in, and I have her correspondence among my collections. Her connection to my efforts shows the breadth of my network in social and religious activism.

Reinhold Niebuhr was another significant figure whose papers I preserved alongside mine. His ideas and correspondence influenced many of the social and religious discussions I was part of. Having his documents helped me understand the theological and ethical debates of the time.

Dorothy Dexter was among the people whose papers I kept because they were closely connected to my work and personal interests. She was part of the network of activists and churchmen I interacted with. Her contributions were important enough that I made sure to save her documents.

I was born in Martinsville, Virginia, which is located in Henry County just across the line from North Carolina. It was my early home before we moved due to my father's financial difficulties. Martinsville was part of my southern roots and family history.

Henry County, Virginia, is where Martinsville is located, and that's where I was born in 1904. It sits just across the border from North Carolina, grounding my family's southern heritage. This county was part of the landscape of my childhood and family stories.

North Carolina is just across the line from Henry County, Virginia, where I was born. Although I didn't grow up there, its proximity was part of the regional context of my early life. My family's roots and movements were influenced by the borderlands between Virginia and North Carolina.

My maternal grandfather was the manager of the John Daniel Plantation, which was partly in Amherst County near Sweet Briar College and also in Campbell County near Lynchburg. This plantation was a significant part of my family background and southern heritage. It connected me to the land and history of Virginia.

Amherst County is where part of the John Daniel Plantation was located, near Sweet Briar College. This area was part of my family's history through my grandfather's management of the plantation. It represents an important geographical and familial connection in Virginia.

Sweet Briar College is near the part of Amherst County where the John Daniel Plantation was located, which my grandfather managed. Though I did not attend Sweet Briar, it is part of the landscape of my family's history. It situates my family's roots in Virginia's educational and agricultural context.

Campbell County, near Lynchburg, Virginia, was another location of the John Daniel Plantation managed by my grandfather. This county was part of the broader area tied to my family's plantation and heritage. It connects my family to the Lynchburg region and its history.

Lynchburg, Virginia, is near Campbell County where part of my grandfather's plantation was located. I also attended Lynchburg College, where I earned my A.B. degree and studied to be a preacher. Lynchburg was a central place in both my family background and my education.

On my father's side, the Kester family lived around Cologne in Germany. They were tailors by trade, a profession that had been in the family for generations. My father wanted to be a mining engineer, but his father insisted he continue the tailoring tradition established in Cologne.

My ancestors, who were Quakers, left Germany and went to England where they connected with William Penn. Penn encouraged them to survey parts of Pennsylvania, and they were given land there, including areas near Philadelphia. This connection to William Penn is a proud part of my family's Quaker heritage.

Pennsylvania is where my Quaker ancestors settled after leaving Germany and England, encouraged by William Penn. They surveyed parts of the state and received land grants near Philadelphia and other places like Williamsburg or Williamstown. This state is important in my family's history and their stand against slavery.

Philadelphia holds significance for my family because a plaque there commemorates my ancestor Praetorius, who signed the first written protest against slavery in America. It is a place that symbolizes my family's Quaker heritage and their early stand for justice. I learned about this only years after growing up.

My father was a member of the Ku Klux Klan, though not a leader. My mother discovered his Klan regalia, which nearly broke her heart. Despite this, my father was respected in the community and did not engage in violence, but his membership was a complicated part of my family history.

Williamsburg, Pennsylvania, or possibly Williamstown, was one of the places where my Quaker ancestors received land grants from William Penn. This location is part of the family lore about their settlement and surveying work in Pennsylvania. It ties into the broader story of my family's migration and land ownership.

My father attended a Cutting School in New York City to become a first-rate tailor. This training was part of the family tradition in tailoring that he was expected to continue despite his own wishes to be an engineer. New York City was where he gained the skills that defined his professional life.

Before I was born, my family lived in Bristol, Virginia, where my father worked as a tailor. This was one of several places we lived before settling in Martinsville and later moving to Beckley, West Virginia. Bristol was part of my early family movements in the South.

Roanoke, Virginia, was another city where my family lived before I was born. My father continued his tailoring work there as we moved around the region. Roanoke was part of the southern towns that shaped my family's early years.

We moved to West Virginia, specifically Beckley in Raleigh County, after my father ran into financial difficulties. Beckley was a mining community where my sister still owns the old home place. This move marked a significant change in our family's circumstances and location.

Beckley, West Virginia, is where my family settled after leaving Virginia due to financial troubles. It is a mining community where my sister now owns the old home place. Beckley became our new home and represents a chapter of my family's life in West Virginia.

My brother retired and now lives near Orlando, Florida. Although I did not live there myself, it is part of my immediate family's current locations. Orlando represents where some of my family members settled later in life.

I attended Lynchburg College where I earned my A.B. degree and certificates in Bible and Greek, preparing to be a preacher. The Dean was supportive enough to allow me to take two months off for a speaking tour in Europe. Lynchburg College was central to my education and early activism.

In 1923, through the YMCA and the World Student Christian Federation, I was selected to visit war-torn Europe to raise support for European students. David Porter, the National Secretary of the Student YMCA, asked me to be the Regional Director of European Student Relief in the South. The YMCA played a key role in launching my pacifist activism and international work.

The World Student Christian Federation was involved in selecting students, including myself, to visit Europe after World War I to aid war-torn countries. This experience profoundly shaped my pacifist beliefs. It was through this organization that I began my work in interracial and international student relief efforts.

David Porter was the National Secretary of the Student YMCA who visited my college and asked me to become the Regional Director of European Student Relief in the South. His invitation launched my involvement in international humanitarian work. He provided support including a salary, telephone, and secretary, which helped me carry out my duties.

During my two-month speaking tour in 1923, I was a guest of the French government and visited places like Verdun to see the battlefields. The experience of witnessing the devastation of war in France deeply influenced my decision to become a pacifist. France was a key location in my early activism and education about war's horrors.

Verdun was one of the battlefields in France that I visited during my 1923 trip through Europe. I was taken around by the same guide who had shown Woodrow Wilson the battlefield. Seeing the devastation firsthand at Verdun convinced me that I wanted nothing to do with war.

Woodrow Wilson was mentioned in connection with the guide who took me around the Verdun battlefield in 1923. The same man who showed Wilson the battlefield also showed us, which linked my experience to historical events of World War I. Wilson's legacy was part of the context for my reflections on war and peace.

Channing Tobias and William Craner asked me to visit Negro colleges during my speaking tour, emphasizing the importance of including African American students in relief efforts. Their encouragement led me to engage with both white and Negro colleges in the South. Tobias was a key figure in promoting interracial understanding in my work.

William Craner, along with Channing Tobias, urged me to include Negro colleges in my speaking tour despite limited financial support. Their insistence helped me broaden the scope of my work to be more inclusive of African American students. Craner was instrumental in shaping the interracial aspect of my activism.

I was aware of the Interracial Commission and knew Alexander, who was connected to it. Although I can't recall exactly when I met Dr. Will, he was involved with the Commission and was an old Vanderbilt man. The Commission's work intersected with my efforts in interracial student groups and activism.

I got to know Dr. Will fairly well at Vanderbilt University, where Dean Brown invited him annually to speak. Vanderbilt was a place where I encountered different perspectives, including Dr. Will's laughter at my socialism. This university was part of the broader academic and social context of my activism.

Dean Brown was the dean of Lynchburg College who allowed me to take two months off for my speaking tour in 1923. He was supportive, requiring me to keep up with assignments and allowing some flexibility with professors. Dean Brown's support was crucial in enabling my early activism and educational pursuits.
